title: Smallest Rotation of a String
description: Return lexicographically smallest rotation of s. Example: baca -> acab

examples.explanation: String length n=4. Initialize min_rot='baca'. i=0: rot='baca' (s[0:]+s[:0]='baca'+''), compare 'baca' < 'baca'? No (equal). i=1: rot='acab' (s[1:]+s[:1]='aca'+'b'), compare 'acab' < 'baca'? Yes ('a' < 'b'), update min_rot='acab'. i=2: rot='caba' (s[2:]+s[:2]='ca'+'ba'), compare 'caba' < 'acab'? No ('c' > 'a'). i=3: rot='abac' (s[3:]+s[:3]='a'+'bac'), compare 'abac' < 'acab'? Yes (position 1: 'b' < 'c'), update min_rot='abac'. Final min_rot='abac'. Verification: rotations of 'baca' = [baca, acab, caba, abac]. Lex order: abac < acab < baca < caba → smallest is 'abac'.

n=5. min_rot='hello'. i=0: rot='hello', compare 'hello' < 'hello'? No. i=1: rot='elloh', compare 'elloh' < 'hello'? Yes ('e' < 'h'), update min_rot='elloh'. i=2: rot='llohe', compare 'llohe' < 'elloh'? No ('l' > 'e'). i=3: rot='lohel', compare 'lohel' < 'elloh'? No ('l' > 'e'). i=4: rot='ohell', compare 'ohell' < 'elloh'? No ('o' > 'e'). Final min_rot='elloh'.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible rotations of the string by shifting characters one by one, compare them lexicographically to find the smallest. Use a simple loop to create each rotation and track the minimum.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Smallest Rotation
ladders.desc: Find lexicographically smallest rotation of a string by generating all rotations and comparing them.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the smallest rotation problem by systematically exploring all possible rotations of the input string. The intuition is straightforward: if we want to find the smallest rotation, we need to examine every possible rotation and keep track of the lexicographically smallest one we encounter. This works because the solution space is finite (n rotations for a string of length n) and we can generate each rotation by shifting characters. The algorithm starts by initializing the minimum rotation as the original string. Then for each possible starting index i (from 0 to n-1), we construct the rotation that begins at index i by taking the substring from i to end plus the substring from beginning to i-1. We compare this rotation to our current minimum using lexicographic comparison, updating the minimum if the new rotation is smaller. After checking all rotations, we return the minimum. Time complexity is O(n²) because we generate n rotations, each of length n, and comparing two strings of length n takes O(n) time. Space complexity is O(n) for storing the current rotation. This connects to L1 by using the rotation generation as a key subroutine. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle empty strings (should return empty string), not considering all n rotations (some implementations might only check n-1 rotations), and inefficient string concatenation that creates unnecessary intermediate strings. Real code pattern: def smallest_rotation(s): n = len(s); min_rot = s; for i in range(n): rot = s[i:] + s[:i]; if rot < min_rot: min_rot = rot; return min_rot.

examples.trace: String length n=4. Initialize min_rot='baca'. i=0: rot='baca' (s[0:]+s[:0]='baca'+''), compare 'baca' < 'baca'? No (equal). i=1: rot='acab' (s[1:]+s[:1]='aca'+'b'), compare 'acab' < 'baca'? Yes ('a' < 'b'), update min_rot='acab'. i=2: rot='caba' (s[2:]+s[:2]='ca'+'ba'), compare 'caba' < 'acab'? No ('c' > 'a'), no update. i=3: rot='abac' (s[3:]+s[:3]='a'+'bac'), compare 'abac' < 'acab'? Yes (position 1: 'b' < 'c'), update min_rot='abac'. Final min_rot='abac'.

n=5. min_rot='hello'. i=0: rot='hello', compare 'hello' < 'hello'? No. i=1: rot='elloh', compare 'elloh' < 'hello'? Yes ('e' < 'h'), update min_rot='elloh'. i=2: rot='llohe', compare 'llohe' < 'elloh'? No ('l' > 'e'). i=3: rot='lohel', compare 'lohel' < 'elloh'? No ('l' > 'e'). i=4: rot='ohell', compare 'ohell' < 'elloh'? No ('o' > 'e'). Final min_rot='elloh'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate All Rotations
ladders.desc: Generate all n rotations of a string by cyclic shifting.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the brute force approach, responsible for producing every possible rotation of the input string. The intuition is that rotating a string means starting at different positions and wrapping around to the beginning. For a string of length n, there are exactly n distinct rotations (including the original). We can generate rotation i by taking the substring from index i to the end, then appending the substring from the beginning to index i-1. This effectively shifts the string left by i positions. The algorithm iterates i from 0 to n-1, and for each i, constructs rotation = s[i:] + s[:i]. This creates a new string each time. Time complexity is O(n²) overall if we generate all rotations (n rotations each of length n), but the generation of one rotation is O(n) due to string concatenation. Space complexity is O(n) for each rotation string. This feeds into L0 by providing the list of candidates to compare for the smallest. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in the loop range (should be range(n)), incorrect slicing indices, and creating unnecessary copies. Real code pattern: def generate_rotations(s): n = len(s); rotations = []; for i in range(n): rot = s[i:] + s[:i]; rotations.append(rot); return rotations.

examples.trace: n=3. i=0: rot='abc'+''='abc'. i=1: rot='bc'+'a'='bca'. i=2: rot='c'+'ab'='cab'. Result: ['abc', 'bca', 'cab'].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Lexicographic Comparison of Rotations
ladders.desc: Compare two string rotations to determine which is lexicographically smaller.
ladders.explanation: The central technique in finding the smallest rotation is lexicographic (dictionary) comparison. The intuition is that strings can be ordered alphabetically, and we need to find the rotation that comes first in this order. Lexicographic comparison works by comparing characters at each position from left to right. At the first position where characters differ, the string with the smaller character (based on ASCII/Unicode value) is considered smaller. If all characters are equal up to the length of the shorter string, the shorter string is smaller. For rotations of the same length, we compare entire strings. In the brute force approach, we maintain a current minimum rotation and update it whenever we find a rotation that compares as smaller. The algorithm: initialize min_rotation, for each new rotation, if rotation < min_rotation (using language's string comparison), update min_rotation. Time complexity for comparing two strings of length n is O(n) in worst case. Space is O(1) for comparison. This is the core logic used in L1 and L0. Common mistakes: Not understanding lexicographic order (e.g., thinking '10' < '2' which is false because '1' < '2'), or using incorrect comparison operators. Real code pattern: def is_smaller(a, b): return a < b.

examples.trace: Compare 'acab' and 'abac' character by character: position 0: 'a' == 'a'. position 1: 'c' vs 'b', 'b' < 'c', so 'abac' < 'acab'. Thus 'abac' is smaller.
Compare 'hello' and 'elloh': position 0: 'h' vs 'e', 'e' < 'h', so 'elloh' < 'hello'. Thus 'elloh' is smaller.

ladders.title: Building Block — String Rotation by Index
ladders.desc: Generate a single rotation of a string given a start index.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves creating one specific rotation of a string by starting at a given index and wrapping around. The intuition is that rotating a string shifts its characters cyclically. For a string s of length n and a start index i (0 ≤ i < n), the rotation starting at i is formed by taking the substring from i to the end, then appending the substring from the beginning to i-1. This can be done using string slicing: rotation = s[i:] + s[:i]. For example, if s='hello' and i=2, rotation = 'llo' + 'he' = 'llohe'. The algorithm is straightforward: compute n = len(s), check that i is in range, then return s[i:] + s[:i]. Time complexity is O(n) because slicing creates new strings of length up to n. Space complexity is O(n) for the new string. This building block is used in L2 to generate individual rotations for comparison. Common mistakes: Using incorrect slice indices (e.g., s[i:] + s[i:] which duplicates), not handling i=0 or i=n-1 correctly, and assuming rotation in-place without creating new string. Real code pattern: def rotate_string(s, i): return s[i:] + s[:i].

examples.trace: n=5. s[3:] = 'ld', s[:3] = 'wor'. Concatenate: 'ld' + 'wor' = 'ldwor'.
n=3. s[0:] = 'abc', s[:0] = ''. Concatenate: 'abc' + '' = 'abc'.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Slicing and Concatenation
ladders.desc: Extract substrings and combine strings to form new strings.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves manipulating strings by taking parts (slicing) and joining them (concatenation). The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we can access portions using indices. Slicing s[start:end] extracts characters from index start (inclusive) to end (exclusive). If start is omitted, it defaults to 0; if end is omitted, it defaults to the end. Concatenation uses the + operator to join two strings end-to-end. For example, for s='hello', s[1:4] = 'ell', and s[1:] + s[:1] = 'ello' + 'h' = 'elloh'. The algorithm for rotation uses slicing to get two parts and concatenation to combine them. Time complexity for slicing is O(k) where k is the length of the slice, as it creates a new string. Concatenation is O(m+n) for strings of length m and n. Space complexity is O(n) for the new string. This operation is used in L3 to build rotations. Common mistakes: Confusing slice indices (Python uses 0-based indexing, exclusive end), forgetting that slicing creates copies, and inefficient concatenation in loops (better to use list join). Real code pattern: part1 = s[i:]; part2 = s[:i]; result = part1 + part2.

examples.trace: For 'abcdef', indices: 0:a, 1:b, 2:c, 3:d, 4:e, 5:f. s[2:5] extracts indices 2,3,4 → 'cde'. Concatenate 'cde' + 'xy' = 'cdexy'.
s[1:] = 'ello', s[:1] = 'h'. Concatenate 'ello' + 'h' = 'elloh'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Strings as Sequences
ladders.desc: Understand strings as ordered collections of characters with index access.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: strings are sequences of characters where each character has a position (index). The intuition is that a string like 'cat' can be thought of as a list: position 0 is 'c', position 1 is 'a', position 2 is 't'. We can access individual characters using indices, e.g., s[0] returns the first character. Strings have a length, given by len(s), which tells how many characters they contain. In Python and many languages, indices start at 0. This foundational idea is crucial because rotations involve accessing different parts of the string by index. The algorithm for understanding this: define a string, use len() to get its length, and access s[i] for valid i. Time complexity for index access is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for accessing. This feeds into L4 for slicing operations. Common mistakes: Using 1-based indexing (common for beginners), accessing out-of-range indices (causes error), and not understanding that strings are immutable (cannot change individual characters). Real code pattern: s = 'hello'; n = len(s); first_char = s[0]; last_char = s[n-1].

examples.trace: String 'dog' has length len('dog') = 3. Indices: 0 -> 'd', 1 -> 'o', 2 -> 'g'. Access: s[0] = 'd', s[1] = 'o', s[2] = 'g'.
String 'a' has length 1. Index 0 -> 'a'. s[0] = 'a'.

approaches.name: Duplicate String and Sliding Window O(n)
approaches.summary: Concatenate the string to itself to form a 2n-length string, then use a sliding window of size n to compare all rotations efficiently. This avoids explicit rotation generation by leveraging the duplicated structure.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Lexicographically Smallest Rotation Using Duplicated String
ladders.desc: Implement complete O(n) solution to find smallest rotation by duplicating string and comparing windows.
ladders.explanation:
This problem requires finding the lexicographically smallest rotation of a string s. A naive approach would generate all n rotations and compare them, taking O(n²) time. The efficient approach leverages string duplication to create a 2n-length string s+s, where every possible rotation of s appears as a contiguous substring of length n starting at positions 0 through n-1. By comparing these n windows efficiently, we can find the starting index of the smallest rotation without explicitly generating rotations.

INTUITION: When we duplicate s to create s+s, the substring from i to i+n-1 (for 0 ≤ i < n) represents the rotation starting at character i of the original string. This allows us to examine all rotations as contiguous substrings in a single structure.

ALGORITHM:
1. Create duplicated string t = s + s (length 2n)
2. Initialize best_start = 0 (assume rotation starting at index 0 is smallest)
3. For i from 1 to n-1:
   a. Compare substring starting at best_start with substring starting at i
   b. Use while loop to compare character by character until difference found
   c. If substring at i is smaller, update best_start = i
4. Return substring of s from best_start to end + beginning to best_start-1

TIME/SPACE ANALYSIS: Time O(n) because each character comparison either advances the comparison pointer or eliminates a candidate. Space O(n) for the duplicated string.

CONNECTION TO L1: This uses the window comparison subroutine from L1 to efficiently compare rotations.

COMMON MISTAKES:
1. Forgetting to handle wrap-around when constructing final result
2. Not optimizing comparison to avoid O(n²) worst case
3. Off-by-one errors in loop boundaries

REAL CODE PATTERN:
def smallest_rotation(s):
    n = len(s)
    t = s + s
    best = 0
    
    for i in range(1, n):
        k = 0
        while k < n and t[best + k] == t[i + k]:
            k += 1
        if k < n and t[best + k] > t[i + k]:
            best = i
    
    return s[best:] + s[:best]

examples.trace:
s = 'baca', n = 4, t = 'bacabaca'
Initialize best = 0 (rotation 'baca')
i = 1: Compare t[0:4]='baca' vs t[1:5]='acab'
  k=0: 'b' vs 'a' → 'b' > 'a', so best = 1
  Current best rotation: t[1:5]='acab'
i = 2: Compare t[1:5]='acab' vs t[2:6]='caba'
  k=0: 'a' vs 'c' → 'a' < 'c', no change
  Current best: 'acab'
i = 3: Compare t[1:5]='acab' vs t[3:7]='abac'
  k=0: 'a' vs 'a' equal
  k=1: 'c' vs 'b' → 'c' > 'b', so best = 3
  Current best: t[3:7]='abac'
All i checked. Return s[3:] + s[:3] = 'a' + 'bac' = 'abac'

s = 'abracadabra', n = 11, t = 'abracadabraabracadabra'
Initialize best = 0 (rotation 'abracadabra')
i = 1: Compare 'abracadabra' vs 'bracadabraa'
  k=0: 'a' vs 'b' → 'a' < 'b', no change
  Current best: 'abracadabra'
i = 2: Compare 'abracadabra' vs 'racadabraab'
  k=0: 'a' vs 'r' → 'a' < 'r', no change
... continue through i = 10
All comparisons show 'abracadabra' is smallest
Return original string unchanged

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compare Two Rotations in Duplicated String
ladders.desc: Compare two n-length windows in duplicated string to determine lexicographic order.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the core comparison engine for finding the smallest rotation. Given a duplicated string t = s+s and two starting indices i and j (both < n), we need to determine whether the rotation starting at i is lexicographically smaller than, equal to, or greater than the rotation starting at j.

INTUITION: Since both windows represent rotations of the same original string, we compare them character by character. The first position where they differ determines their relative order. If we reach the end without finding a difference, the rotations are identical.

ALGORITHM:
1. Initialize k = 0
2. While k < n:
   a. If t[i+k] < t[j+k], return -1 (i is smaller)
   b. If t[i+k] > t[j+k], return 1 (j is smaller)
   c. k += 1
3. Return 0 (rotations are equal)

TIME/SPACE ANALYSIS: Worst case O(n) when rotations are identical or nearly identical. Average case much better. Space O(1).

CONNECTION TO L0: This comparison function is called repeatedly in the main algorithm to compare candidate rotations.

COMMON MISTAKES:
1. Not handling the case where windows are identical
2. Comparing beyond n characters
3. Not optimizing with early termination

REAL CODE PATTERN:
def compare_rotations(t, i, j, n):
    for k in range(n):
        if t[i+k] < t[j+k]:
            return -1
        elif t[i+k] > t[j+k]:
            return 1
    return 0

examples.trace:
t = 'bacabaca', n = 4
Compare windows starting at i=0 and j=1
k=0: t[0]='b', t[1]='a' → 'b' > 'a'
Immediately return 1 (j window is smaller)
Rotation 'acab' is smaller than 'baca'

t = 'abcabc', n = 3
Compare windows starting at i=0 and j=3
k=0: t[0]='a', t[3]='a' equal
k=1: t[1]='b', t[4]='b' equal
k=2: t[2]='c', t[5]='c' equal
All characters match, return 0
Rotations 'abc' and 'abc' are identical

ladders.title: Core Logic — Representing Rotations as Windows in Duplicated String
ladders.desc: Understand how string duplication captures all rotations as contiguous substrings.
ladders.explanation:
This is the central insight that makes the O(n) algorithm possible. By concatenating a string with itself, we create a structure where every possible rotation appears as a contiguous substring.

INTUITION: A rotation of string s is obtained by taking some suffix of s and appending the corresponding prefix. When we create t = s+s, any substring of length n starting at position i (where 0 ≤ i < n) contains exactly: characters i through n-1 from s (the suffix), followed by characters 0 through i-1 from s (the prefix). This is precisely the rotation starting at position i.

MATHEMATICAL INSIGHT: For string s of length n, rotation starting at index i is: s[i:] + s[:i]. In t = s+s, the substring t[i:i+n] = s[i:] + s[:i].

EXAMPLE: s = 'abcd', n = 4, t = 'abcdabcd'
- i=0: t[0:4] = 'abcd' = s[0:] + s[:0] = 'abcd' + ''
- i=1: t[1:5] = 'bcda' = s[1:] + s[:1] = 'bcd' + 'a'
- i=2: t[2:6] = 'cdab' = s[2:] + s[:2] = 'cd' + 'ab'
- i=3: t[3:7] = 'dabc' = s[3:] + s[:3] = 'd' + 'abc'

TIME/SPACE ANALYSIS: Creating t takes O(n) time and O(n) space.

CONNECTION TO L1: This duplicated string provides the structure where L1's comparison operates.

COMMON MISTAKES:
1. Creating t with wrong length (should be exactly 2n)
2. Not understanding why all rotations are captured
3. Confusing rotation index with substring position

REAL CODE PATTERN:
# This is the insight, not a function
t = s + s  # All rotations are now t[i:i+n] for 0 ≤ i < n

examples.trace:
s = 'baca', length n = 4
Create t = s + s = 'baca' + 'baca' = 'bacabaca'
All rotations as substrings:
- i=0: t[0:4] = 'baca' (original)
- i=1: t[1:5] = 'acab' (rotate left by 1)
- i=2: t[2:6] = 'caba' (rotate left by 2)
- i=3: t[3:7] = 'abac' (rotate left by 3)
These are all 4 possible rotations

s = 'ab', length n = 2
Create t = s + s = 'ab' + 'ab' = 'abab'
All rotations as substrings:
- i=0: t[0:2] = 'ab' (original)
- i=1: t[1:3] = 'ba' (rotate left by 1)
These are both possible rotations

ladders.title: Building Block — Lexicographic String Comparison
ladders.desc: Compare two strings character by character to determine their dictionary order.
ladders.explanation:
Lexicographic comparison (also called dictionary order) is fundamental to this problem. We need to determine which of two strings comes first in alphabetical order.

INTUITION: Compare strings character by character from left to right. At the first position where characters differ, the string with the smaller character (according to ASCII/Unicode) is lexicographically smaller. If one string is a prefix of the other, the shorter string is smaller.

ALGORITHM:
1. Let i = 0
2. While i < min(len(a), len(b)):
   a. If a[i] < b[i], return -1 (a is smaller)
   b. If a[i] > b[i], return 1 (b is smaller)
   c. i += 1
3. If len(a) < len(b), return -1
4. If len(a) > len(b), return 1
5. Return 0 (strings are equal)

TIME/SPACE ANALYSIS: O(min(m,n)) where m,n are string lengths. Space O(1).

CONNECTION TO L2: This comparison is what we apply to the windows in the duplicated string.

COMMON MISTAKES:
1. Not handling different length strings correctly
2. Comparing beyond string boundaries
3. Confusing lexicographic with numeric comparison

REAL CODE PATTERN:
def lexicographic_compare(a, b):
    min_len = min(len(a), len(b))
    for i in range(min_len):
        if a[i] < b[i]:
            return -1
        elif a[i] > b[i]:
            return 1
    if len(a) < len(b):
        return -1
    elif len(a) > len(b):
        return 1
    return 0

examples.trace:
Compare 'apple' and 'banana'
Position 0: 'a' vs 'b' → 'a' < 'b'
Immediately return -1
'apple' comes before 'banana' in dictionary

Compare 'cat' and 'catalog'
Position 0: 'c' vs 'c' equal
Position 1: 'a' vs 'a' equal
Position 2: 't' vs 't' equal
'cat' ends here, 'catalog' continues
Since 'cat' is shorter and prefix of 'catalog', return -1
'cat' comes before 'catalog'

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Concatenation and Slicing
ladders.desc: Create new strings by joining and extracting substrings.
ladders.explanation:
String concatenation (joining strings) and slicing (extracting substrings) are fundamental operations needed for this algorithm. Concatenation creates a new string by appending one string to another. Slicing extracts a contiguous portion of a string.

INTUITION: Strings are immutable sequences of characters. Concatenation creates a new string containing all characters from the first string followed by all characters from the second. Slicing uses indices to specify a start (inclusive) and end (exclusive) position.

OPERATIONS:
1. Concatenation: result = s1 + s2
   - Creates new string of length len(s1) + len(s2)
   - Example: 'hello' + 'world' = 'helloworld'

2. Slicing: substring = s[start:end]
   - Extracts characters from index start to end-1
   - If start omitted, defaults to 0
   - If end omitted, defaults to len(s)
   - Example: 'hello'[1:4] = 'ell'

TIME/SPACE ANALYSIS: Both operations take O(k) time where k is length of result. Space O(k) for new string.

CONNECTION TO L3: These operations are used to create the duplicated string and extract rotations.

COMMON MISTAKES:
1. Forgetting that string indices start at 0
2. Confusing inclusive/exclusive bounds in slicing
3. Not realizing concatenation creates new string (not modify in-place)

REAL CODE PATTERN:
# Concatenation
t = s + s  # Double the string

# Slicing
rotation = s[i:] + s[:i]  # Rotation starting at i
substring = t[start:start+n]  # n characters from t

examples.trace:
s = 'code'
Concatenation: s + s
Step 1: Take all characters of first 'c','o','d','e'
Step 2: Append all characters of second 'c','o','d','e'
Result: 'c','o','d','e','c','o','d','e' = 'codecode'

s = 'algorithm'
Indices: 0:a, 1:l, 2:g, 3:o, 4:r, 5:i, 6:t, 7:h, 8:m
Slice s[2:6]:
Start at index 2 (inclusive): 'g'
End at index 6 (exclusive): stop before 't'
Characters: index 2:'g', 3:'o', 4:'r', 5:'i'
Result: 'gori'

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Rotation Basics
ladders.desc: Understand what a string rotation is and how to generate them.
ladders.explanation:
A rotation of a string is obtained by moving some characters from the beginning to the end, or vice versa, while maintaining the relative order of characters. Think of it as taking the string and rotating it circularly.

INTUITION: If you write a string in a circle, any starting point gives you a rotation. For a string of length n, there are exactly n distinct rotations (including the original).

FORMAL DEFINITION: For string s of length n, a rotation by k positions (0 ≤ k < n) is: s[k:] + s[:k]
- s[k:] means characters from position k to the end
- s[:k] means characters from beginning to position k-1

EXAMPLE: s = 'abcd', n = 4
- Rotation 0: s[0:] + s[:0] = 'abcd' + '' = 'abcd' (original)
- Rotation 1: s[1:] + s[:1] = 'bcd' + 'a' = 'bcda'
- Rotation 2: s[2:] + s[:2] = 'cd' + 'ab' = 'cdab'
- Rotation 3: s[3:] + s[:3] = 'd' + 'abc' = 'dabc'

VISUALIZATION: Write 'abcd' in circle:
   a
 d   b
   c
Starting at any letter and reading clockwise gives a rotation.

TIME/SPACE ANALYSIS: Generating one rotation takes O(n) time and O(n) space.

CONNECTION TO L4: Rotations are created using concatenation and slicing operations.

COMMON MISTAKES:
1. Thinking rotations include reversed strings
2. Confusing rotation with permutation
3. Not understanding there are exactly n rotations

REAL CODE PATTERN:
def generate_rotation(s, k):
    n = len(s)
    k = k % n  # Handle k >= n
    return s[k:] + s[:k]

examples.trace:
s = 'hello', length 5
Rotation by k=2:
s[2:] = characters from index 2 to end: 'llo'
s[:2] = characters from start to index 1: 'he'
Concatenate: 'llo' + 'he' = 'llohe'
Visual: hello → move first 2 chars to end

approaches.name: Booth's Algorithm O(n)
approaches.summary: Apply Booth's algorithm, which uses a failure function similar to KMP to find the lexicographically smallest rotation in linear time. It processes the string in a single pass by maintaining pointers to track potential minimal rotations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Smallest Rotation of a String using Booth's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement Booth's algorithm to find the lexicographically smallest rotation of a string in O(n) time.
ladders.explanation: Booth's algorithm finds the lexicographically smallest rotation of a string by treating it as a circular string and using a failure function similar to KMP. The intuition is to compare rotations efficiently without generating all n rotations. We duplicate the string to handle circularity (s + s) and maintain two pointers: i (current candidate for smallest rotation start) and j (comparison index). The algorithm processes the string in a single pass: for each character, if s[i + k] < s[j + k], we advance j to j + k + 1; if s[i + k] > s[j + k], we advance i to i + k + 1; if equal, we increment k. This ensures we skip unnecessary comparisons, achieving O(n) time. The result is the substring of the original string starting at index i mod n. Space is O(n) for the duplicated string. Common mistakes include not handling the modulo operation correctly for the result index or incorrect pointer updates. This directly implements the complete solution.

examples.trace: String: baca, Duplicated: bacabaca, n=4. Initialize i=0, j=1, k=0. Compare s[0+0]=b vs s[1+0]=a: b > a, so set i=0+0+1=1. Reset k=0. Now i=1, j=2. Compare s[1+0]=a vs s[2+0]=c: a < c, so set j=2+0+1=3. Reset k=0. Now i=1, j=3. Compare s[1+0]=a vs s[3+0]=a: equal, k=1. Compare s[1+1]=c vs s[3+1]=b: c > b, so set i=1+1+1=3. Reset k=0. Now i=3, j=4. Compare s[3+0]=a vs s[4+0]=b: a < b, so set j=4+0+1=5. Reset k=0. Now i=3, j=5 (j=5 < n+i=7, continue). Compare s[3+0]=a vs s[5+0]=a: equal, k=1. Compare s[3+1]=b vs s[5+1]=c: b < c, so set j=5+1+1=7. Reset k=0. Now j=7 >= n+i=7, stop. Result index = i=3. Rotation: s[3:]+s[:3] = 'a'+'bac' = 'abac'.

String: ababa, Duplicated: ababaababa, n=5. Initialize i=0, j=1, k=0. Compare s[0+0]=a vs s[1+0]=b: a < b, so set j=1+0+1=2. Reset k=0. Now i=0, j=2. Compare s[0+0]=a vs s[2+0]=a: equal, k=1. Compare s[0+1]=b vs s[2+1]=b: equal, k=2. Compare s[0+2]=a vs s[2+2]=a: equal, k=3. Compare s[0+3]=b vs s[2+3]=b: equal, k=4. Compare s[0+4]=a vs s[2+4]=a: equal, k=5. k reaches n, stop. Result index = min(i,j) = min(0,2)=0. Rotation: original string ababa is already smallest? Wait, check: rotations: ababa, babaa, abaab, baaba, aabab. Lex smallest is aabab. Algorithm gave index 0, but that's incorrect? Let's trace carefully: After k=5, we have compared full length, so set j=2+5=7? Actually, when k=n, we break and result is min(i,j)=0. But expected is aabab (index 4). Error in algorithm? Booth's algorithm should handle this. Let's re-trace with correct Booth's: i=0, j=1, k=0. s[0]=a, s[1]=b: a<b, so j=2. i=0, j=2, k=0. s[0]=a, s[2]=a: equal, k=1. s[1]=b, s[3]=b: equal, k=2. s[2]=a, s[4]=a: equal, k=3. s[3]=b, s[5]=a: b>a, so set i=0+3+1=4. Reset k=0. Now i=4, j=5. s[4]=a, s[5]=a: equal, k=1. s[5]=a, s[6]=b: a<b, so set j=5+1+1=7. Reset k=0. j=7 >= n+i=9, stop. Result index = min(i,j)=4. Rotation: s[4:]+s[:4]=a+abab=aabab. Correct.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Failure Function for Lexicographic Comparison in Booth's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement the failure function that efficiently compares rotations and updates pointers in Booth's algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of Booth's algorithm, responsible for comparing two candidate rotations starting at indices i and j in the duplicated string s+s. The intuition is to avoid redundant comparisons by using a failure function similar to KMP: when characters match, we extend the comparison; when they differ, we skip ahead based on lexicographic order. The function takes i, j, k as input and updates them. Steps: while i < n and j < n and k < n, compare s[i+k] and s[j+k]. If s[i+k] < s[j+k], rotation starting at j cannot be smaller, so set j = j + k + 1 and reset k=0. If s[i+k] > s[j+k], rotation starting at i cannot be smaller, so set i = i + k + 1 and reset k=0. If equal, increment k. This ensures O(n) time as each mismatch advances i or j by at least k+1, and matches increase k. Space is O(1) for pointers. Common mistakes include not resetting k after pointer updates or incorrect comparison logic. This feeds into L0 by being the main loop in Booth's algorithm.

examples.trace: s='bacabaca', n=4 (original length), but we use duplicated length 8 for indices. Start i=0, j=1, k=0. Compare s[0]=b vs s[1]=a: b > a, so set i = 0 + 0 + 1 = 1. Reset k=0. Now i=1, j=1, k=0. Return updated values.
s='ababaababa', n=5. Start i=0, j=1, k=0. Compare s[0]=a vs s[1]=b: a < b, so set j = 1 + 0 + 1 = 2. Reset k=0. Now i=0, j=2, k=0. Return updated values.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Lexicographic Comparison of Rotations with Skipping
ladders.desc: Understand how to compare two rotations efficiently by skipping unnecessary indices based on character comparisons.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that when comparing two rotations starting at indices i and j, if we find a mismatch at position k (i.e., s[i+k] != s[j+k]), we can deduce which rotation is lexicographically smaller and skip ahead. Specifically, if s[i+k] < s[j+k], then the rotation starting at j cannot be the smallest because at the first differing character, the i rotation is smaller. Thus, we can advance j to j+k+1 to skip all rotations between j and j+k. Similarly, if s[i+k] > s[j+k], we advance i to i+k+1. If characters are equal, we continue comparing. This skipping mechanism ensures each character is compared at most twice, leading to O(n) time. The intuition is similar to the KMP failure function but applied to lexicographic order. This technique is used in L1's failure function. Common mistakes include not adding 1 when skipping or incorrectly handling equal characters. Real code pattern: while k < n and i < n and j < n: if s[i+k] < s[j+k]: j += k+1; k=0; elif s[i+k] > s[j+k]: i += k+1; k=0; else: k+=1.

examples.input: Compare rotations starting at i=0 and j=1 in 'baca' duplicated as 'bacabaca'
examples.trace: Duplicated string: bacabaca. Rotation i: starting at index 0: b a c a. Rotation j: starting at index 1: a c a b. Compare first char: b vs a. b > a, so rotation i is larger. Thus, skip i to 0+0+1=1. Now compare rotations starting at i=1 and j=1? Actually, after skip, we reset and compare new i=1 with j=1. But in algorithm, we update i and reset. This shows skipping: since b>a, we know rotation at i=0 cannot be smallest, so we skip it.

examples.input: Compare rotations starting at i=0 and j=1 in 'ababa' duplicated as 'ababaababa'
examples.trace: Duplicated string: ababaababa. Rotation i: a b a b a. Rotation j: b a b a a. Compare first char: a vs b. a < b, so rotation j is larger. Thus, skip j to 1+0+1=2. Now j=2, rotation starting at index 2: a b a a b. Continue comparison.

ladders.title: Building Block — Duplicating String for Circular Comparisons
ladders.desc: Learn how to handle circular strings by duplicating the string to simulate rotations.
ladders.explanation: To find the smallest rotation, we need to compare all n rotations of the string. A rotation is obtained by taking a substring from some index to the end and then from the start to that index. Instead of generating all rotations explicitly (which would take O(n²) time), we duplicate the string: create s' = s + s. Now, any rotation starting at index i in the original string corresponds to the substring s'[i : i+n]. This allows us to compare rotations by simply indexing into s' without modular arithmetic. The intuition is that a circular string can be linearized by concatenating it with itself. For example, for s='baca', s'='bacabaca'. Rotation starting at index 1 is 'acab', which is s'[1:5]. This building block is used in L2 and L1 to access characters for comparison. Time to duplicate is O(n), space is O(n). Common mistakes include not duplicating or using incorrect indices. Real code pattern: duplicated = s + s. This feeds into higher levels by providing the extended string for Booth's algorithm.

examples.trace: Original string: baca, length n=4. Duplicate: concatenate s with itself: baca + baca = bacabaca. Now, rotations: index 0: s'[0:4]=baca, index 1: s'[1:5]=acab, index 2: s'[2:6]=caba, index 3: s'[3:7]=abac. All rotations are represented.
Original string: ab, length n=2. Duplicate: ab + ab = abab. Rotations: index 0: s'[0:2]=ab, index 1: s'[1:3]=ba.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Lexicographic Comparison of Two Strings
ladders.explanation: Lexicographic comparison is the foundation for determining the smallest rotation. Given two strings a and b, we compare them character by character from left to right. At the first index where a[i] != b[i], if a[i] < b[i] (in ASCII or Unicode order), then a is lexicographically smaller; if a[i] > b[i], then b is smaller. If all characters are equal up to the length of the shorter string, the shorter string is smaller. For rotations, strings are of equal length, so if all characters match, they are equal. The intuition is similar to how words are ordered in a dictionary. This operation is used in L3 and L2 to compare rotations. Time is O(min(len(a), len(b))), space O(1). Common mistakes include not handling different lengths correctly or using incorrect comparison operators. Real code pattern: for i in range(min(len(a), len(b))): if a[i] < b[i]: return -1; elif a[i] > b[i]: return 1; then compare lengths. For rotations, lengths are equal, so just compare characters.

examples.trace: Compare first char: 'a' vs 'b'. 'a' < 'b' in ASCII (97 < 98). So at first mismatch, a is lexicographically smaller. No need to compare further.
Compare char by char: index 0: 'c' vs 'c' equal. index 1: 'a' vs 'a' equal. index 2: 't' vs 'r'. 't' > 'r' (116 > 114), so b is smaller.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Rotation and Circular Strings
ladders.desc: Understand what a string rotation is and how to represent it.
ladders.explanation: A rotation of a string is obtained by moving some characters from the start to the end. Formally, for a string s of length n, a rotation by k positions (0 ≤ k < n) is s[k:] + s[:k]. For example, for s='hello', rotation by 2 is 'llohe'. All rotations form a circular string, where the end connects to the start. The lexicographically smallest rotation is the one that comes first in dictionary order among all rotations. This concept is foundational because the problem asks to find this smallest rotation. To work with rotations, we can think of the string as circular: after the last character, we wrap around to the first. This leads to the idea of duplicating the string for linear access. Understanding rotations is prerequisite for L4 and above. Common mistakes include confusing rotation with reversal or substring. Real code pattern: rotation_k = s[k:] + s[:k]. This is the simplest concept needed to approach the problem.

examples.trace: String length n=3. Rotation k=0: s[0:]+s[:0] = 'abc' + '' = 'abc'. Rotation k=1: s[1:]+s[:1] = 'bc' + 'a' = 'bca'. Rotation k=2: s[2:]+s[:2] = 'c' + 'ab' = 'cab'. So rotations: abc, bca, cab.
